title: Introduction and Scope of Pedestrian Crossing Behavior Research
objective: Introduce the significance of pedestrian safety and define the scope of this review, which synthesizes findings on behavioral and distraction factors influencing pedestrian road-crossing decisions

title: Theoretical Foundations: Theory of Planned Behavior in Pedestrian Crossing
objective: Review the application of the Theory of Planned Behavior framework to predict pedestrian violating crossing intentions, including its core constructs and extensions

title: Behavioral and Social Factors Influencing Crossing Intentions
objective: Synthesize evidence on key behavioral predictors including instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, subjective norm, and conformity tendency from TPB-based studies

title: Risk Perception and Control Factors in Crossing Behavior
objective: Examine the role of perceived risk and perceived behavioral control as predictors of pedestrian crossing behavior intentions

title: Mobile Phone Distraction Effects on Pedestrian Crossing Performance
objective: Synthesize experimental findings on how different types of mobile phone use (texting, conversation, music) affect crossing behavior and visual attention allocation

title: Visual Attention Allocation During Street Crossing
objective: Review eye-tracking evidence on how distraction affects pupil diameter, scanning frequency, fixation patterns, and attention distribution across traffic environments

title: Methodological Approaches and Their Contributions
objective: Compare and contrast the structural equation modeling approach from survey-based TPB studies with controlled laboratory eye-tracking experiments in understanding pedestrian behavior

title: Implications for Pedestrian Safety Interventions
objective: Synthesize practical implications from both research streams for developing effective safety education and infrastructure design strategies
